title: Move semantics basics

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Move semantics basics; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Moving a vector into an owning object allows its buffer to be transferred, while the source vector remains valid for destruction or assignment. Your goal is to trace copy versus move operations and use standard moves only when ownership transfer is intended. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and initialization; Value, reference and const-reference parameters; Copy and assignment semantics; RAII and deterministic cleanup. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Move operations transfer or reuse resources from an expiring object, after which the source remains valid but its state is generally unspecified unless the type documents more. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: std::move is a cast and performs no transfer itself, const objects often cannot move resources, and self-move or exception guarantees need type-specific care. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: std::move immediately empties an object and guarantees exactly which value remains in the source.

includes: Recognize moved-from valid states and allow efficient resource transfer without premature optimization.
Trace copy versus move operations and use standard moves only when ownership transfer is intended.
excludes: std::move is a cast and performs no transfer itself, const objects often cannot move resources, and self-move or exception guarantees need type-specific care.

outcomes: Trace copy versus move operations and use standard moves only when ownership transfer is intended.

examples.title: Move semantics basics: defined case
examples.situation: Moving a vector into an owning object allows its buffer to be transferred, while the source vector remains valid for destruction or assignment.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: Moving a vector into an owning object allows its buffer to be transferred, while the source vector remains valid for destruction or assignment.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: std::move only enables move overload selection; after an actual move, rely only on the source being valid unless a stronger state is documented.
examples.result: The defined result follows because std::move only enables move overload selection; after an actual move, rely only on the source being valid unless a stronger state is documented.

examples.title: Move semantics basics: boundary case
examples.situation: Calling std::move on a const string selects a copy because ordinary move construction requires a non-const rvalue source.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Calling std::move on a const string selects a copy because ordinary move construction requires a non-const rvalue source.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: std::move is a cast and performs no transfer itself, const objects often cannot move resources, and self-move or exception guarantees need type-specific care.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: std::move immediately empties an object and guarantees exactly which value remains in the source.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace copy versus move operations and use standard moves only when ownership transfer is intended.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: std::move immediately empties an object and guarantees exactly which value remains in the source.
misconceptions.correction: std::move only enables move overload selection; after an actual move, rely only on the source being valid unless a stronger state is documented.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: std::move immediately empties an object and guarantees exactly which value remains in the source.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Move semantics basics: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
std::move is a cast and performs no transfer itself, const objects often cannot move resources, and self-move or exception guarantees need type-specific care.

Keep this boundary in view: std::move is a cast and performs no transfer itself, const objects often cannot move resources, and self-move or exception guarantees need type-specific care.
